content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Don’t Look Up movie is the most frustratingly accurate, brilliant, and stressful satire I’ve seen in years. ☄️📱

**Score:** 249
**Body:** I finally watched Don’t Look Up on Netflix, and it is easily the most painfully real dark comedy out there. Instead of a typical sci-fi movie where heroes unite to stop a world-ending comet, it's a biting satire about what happens when a global crisis has to compete with viral social media trends, a shallow 24-hour news cycle, and greedy politicians. The star-studded cast—especially Leonardo DiCaprio and Jonah Hill—is phenomenal at making you laugh while simultaneously giving you an existential crisis. If you haven't seen it yet, go in expecting a sharp mirror held up to modern society rather than a standard disaster flick. For those who have watched it: did it make you laugh, or did it just leave you deeply stressed out?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The scene where DiCaprio is losing his mind on the morning show while the hosts ask him about his outfit was the most accurate part. Felt like watching any climate scientist try to get a word in edgewise on cable news. Jonah Hill being an absolute sleaze was just the cherry on top.
2. Thank you your review makes me want to watch it... which I will do later today
  - It's so good I've watched it like 3-4x
    - Good to know
3. I think you'd enjoy The Network, from the 70s. It's not quite as funny, but deals with the media and Capatalism in a similar way, and is just as relevant now as it was 50 years ago. 
4. That may be, but that still doesn't explain why he would charge us for *free snacks*!
5. The movie is hilarious but scary bc it’s pretty accurate. It’s interesting to see how people act at the end of the world and the acting is well done, especially by Leonardo Dicaprio. Seen it multiple times love it
6. “We really did have everything, didn’t we?” My wife and I say to each other at least once a week these days.
7. please don't call it satire, we thought the same of idiocracy .. let's consider it a fair warning instead.
8. I thought it was entertaining, but I was a little confused about how aggressively dumb and relaxed about imminent disaster the people in power were.

Then I Googled it, found out that the meteor was a metaphor for the climate crisis, and it all made sense.

## 2. Movies where the main character rides a motorcycle.

**Score:** 47
**Body:** Looking for a movie where the main character rides a motorcycle at any point. It can be for the majority of the film or for 10 seconds. It would be even better if the movie is pre-2000.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Easy Rider
2. Ghost Rider
3. Terminator 2, maybe 1 but can’t remember it
4. The Great Escape, Mcqueen runs from Nazis on a motorcycle 
5. Tron 
6. Top Gun
7. The World's Fastest Indian 
8. Akira
9. Motorcycle Diaries (2004)
10. A place beyond the pines 

## 3. I’m looking for movie that will make question everything

**Score:** 32
**Body:** The movie I’m looking for is a movie that will make me question everything a movie that will make me think to myself is something wrong with me. It can be a comedy horror,  crime, drama, any type of film, but it must make me question everything a film that will make me stop in my tracks rethink everything have a wonderful day 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Coherence
2. The man from earth... don't read anything about it
3. Being John Malkovich 
4. Arrival 
5. Coherence
6. Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind 
7. Life of Chuck
8. Primer had me scribbling timelines on a napkin halfway through and still not sure if I missed something or the movie just rewrote physics for fun. Kept me up later than a double espresso. Worth a watch if you're ready to feel like your brain's playing a prank on you.
9. The Butterfly Effect
10. Vanilla sky

Trainspotting 

The matrix

## 4. What are some good psychological thriller movies with suspense and twists?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I’m looking for movies that are suspenseful, mysterious, and ominous. I really enjoy watching horror, or anything that is a psychological thriller with some good twists and turns in its plot. Any recommendations?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shutter island 
2. The Game (1997)
3. Frailty 
4. The Lives of Others is a great nonviolent spy thriller. It's a story about a playwright living in the police state of East Germany and the Stasi officer who spies on him. It has a lot of interesting turns.
5. What Lies Beneath 
6. Primal Fear

Arlington Road
7. Jacob's Ladder (1990)
8. Angel Heart
9. Watching Obsession now, seems pretty good so far
10. Saltburn

## 5. Horniest/most sensual (not sexually disturbing) movies you have seen

**Score:** 23
**Body:** So my bf  is coming over tonight and we are planning a movie night and some couple time. i want recommendations that are steamy and yes...horny, like we can actually feel the passion through the screen. 

i dont wan't sexually disturbing films like Irreversible, just keep the suggestions romantic and passionate. Any language, country and year will work.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Thomas Crown Affair (1999).

Definitely steamy but also the heist thriller aspect should keep him engaged if the romance stuff isn't his thing
2. Lady Chatterly’s Lover
3. A Walk on the Moon.  Diane Lane, Viggo Mortensen.  
4. Wild things
5. Unfaithful fits the bill totally, however it is not a happy end.
  - Good film, not a good recommendation for a couples sexy night together.  This is a public service announcement.
6. body heat of course
7. 9 1/2 Weeks 
  - Yes, great pick. Excellent mood, both from a male and female standpoints.
8. Vicky Christina Barcelona

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. How crazy am I for believing that Christian Bale should be considered on the same level of acting talent as Daniel Day Lewis and Gary Oldman?

**Score:** 1230

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Why is The Prestige not on this list? 

Also, yes, Bale is great. 
  - the prestige is probably my favorite movie he's been in. i almost hope op's never seen it and they are in for a treat
  - Seriously, Thor being on this list at all is weird in comparison to The Prestige, 3:10 to Yuma, Equilibrium and Reign of Fire exist.
    - Empire of the sun, rescue dawn and  Harsh tines.
2. Sorry I just have to laugh because Reddit is incapable of mentioning great actors without naming Gary Oldman. If it were a drinking game I’d be dead. 😂 

Anyways, Bale is a master of his craft. Put him in whatever tier you want.
3. I think people will look back on his career and see him as one of the best actors of his generation.
  - https://i.redd.it/zxghtscu6hbh1.gif
4. One of the greatest actors we will  
Ever have. And he drives a 30 year old Toyota Tacoma. He is great.
5. What don't you fucking understand?
6. ![gif](giphy|102kjhy44uF87m)

Been a fan of his since American Psycho. I still consider that his best performance

## 2. What’s your favorite comedy that doesn’t get mentioned often?

**Score:** 929

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'll start, my name is u/hot_rod_kimble and I like to party
  - My name is jfrosty42 and I like to party 
    - Uhh, no jfrosty42, I just said that I like to party, so maybe you could do something different from me
      - My name is jfrosty42 and I am the stunt man
2. Balls of Fury. 

It's hilarious and I'm tired of pretending it's not.
3. Macgruber. 
4. ![gif](giphy|1034EEGrn91SrS)
5. Baseketball.
6. ![gif](giphy|C0ABsPgkTbdrG)
7. in bruges is my go to for this. went in expecting a standard hitman movie and got one of the funniest scripts ive ever heard

## 3. Best Live Action Casting of a Cartoon or Comic Character

**Score:** 581
**Body:** I think Shelley Duvall was born to play Olive Oyl

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. JK Simmons as Jameson
2. Matthew Lillard as Shaggy 
  - Yes! And Linda Cardellini as Velma
3. Professor X = Patrick Stewart 
  - And Magneto = Ian McKellen 
4. Ron Perlman as Hellboy
5. Arnold Schwarzenegger as Conan
6. Mickey Rourke as Marv in Sin City

![gif](giphy|26ufkcwAdhAV5awtq)
7. Without mentioning the quality of the films, I thought Margot Robbie made an *excellent* Harley Quinn, personally.

Also John Goodman as Fred Flintstone.
8. Jackie Earle Haley as Rorschach

## 4. What Are Your Favourite Back-and-Forth Exchanges in Movies? (Janeane Garofalo as the Medieval Waitress and Matthew Broderick as Steven in The Cable Guy)

**Score:** 494
**Body:** ***Steven:*** *Can I get a knife and fork?*

***Medieval Waitress:*** *There were no utensils in medieval times, hence there are no utensils* ***at*** *Medieval Times. Would you like a refill on that Pepsi?*

***Steven:*** *There were no utensils but there was Pepsi?*

***Medieval Waitress:*** *Dude, I got a lotta tables.*

This exchange always gets me because it’s so perfectly delivered by Garofalo. The way she is barely holding up the facade to start, followed by how she completely gives up the act after one follow-up question.

They don’t need to be the funniest… if you’d like to go with the most iconic or most profound, let’s hear ‘em! What are your favourite back-and-forth exchanges in movies?

[https://youtu.be/DujwLnrxlTY?si=peMdJOf2PTqHg28b](https://youtu.be/DujwLnrxlTY?si=peMdJOf2PTqHg28b)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mystery Men (1999)

Mr. Furious: Okay, am I the only one who finds these sayings just a little bit formulaic? "If you want to push something down, you have to pull it up. If you want to go left, you have to go right." It's...

The Sphinx: Your temper is very quick, my friend. But until you learn to master your rage...

Mr. Furious: ...your rage will become your master? That's what you were going to say. Right? Right?

The Sphinx: Not necessarily.
  - so so so good. sometimes my brain just also says, “i’m invisible! can you see me??” “yesssssss” or “lucille, god gave me a gift. i shovel. i shovel well. i shovel very well.” the whole movie, each character, so good 
2. Garofalo is so hilariously understated. 

rewatched Wet Hot the other day and her scenes with David Hyde Pierce were killing me. 
  - She's also pretty amazing in 200 Cigarettes which is one of my favs. 
3. **GROSSE POINT BLANK**         
Marty: Whole grain pancakes and an egg-white omelette, please.       
Waitress: What do you want in your omelette, sir?        
Marty: Nothing in the omelette, nothing at all.        
Waitress: Well, that's not technically an omelette.       
Marty: Look, I don't want to get into a semantic argument, I just want the protein all right.  
  - You can never go home, but you can shop there
4. John Candy and Macaulay Culkin in Uncle Buck
5. When my kids don't pick what they want fast enough I say, "Dude, I got a lot of tables"
6. Casino Royale, the train conversation, first meeting between Bond and Vesper

"How was your lamb.?"

"Skewered. One sympathizes"
7. Paul Rudd and the barista in Role Models:


Barista: Good morning! Can I take your order?

Banks: Can I get a tall chai?

Rudd: And a large black coffee.

Barista: A what?

Rudd: Large black coffee.

Barista: Do you mean a venti?

Rudd: No, I mean a large.

Banks: He means a venti. Yeah, the biggest one you've got.

Barista: Venti means large.

Rudd: No. Venti means "twenty".

Banks: Danny...

Rudd: Yeah. "Large" is large. In fact, "tall" is large, and "grande" is Spanish for large. "Venti" is the only one that doesn't mean large. It's also the only one that's Italian. Congratulations! You're stupid in three languages.

## 5. Constantine is one of the best comic book films of the 2000’s

**Score:** 426
**Body:** Rewatched this gem recently and it still holds up! It’s the perfect supernatural Neo noir flick.

Yes I know that Keanu’s character is a complete deviation from Constantine in the comics. So what? He’s awesome here! I just loved how dry and cynical his character was.

The supporting cast is amazing. Weisz, Swinton, Rossdale, Hounsou, LaBeouf and Stormare are all incredible. But Stormare was out of this world!

Loved the themes the film explores. The meaning of faith, search for redemption, self sacrifice, etc.

One of the best comic book flicks of the 2000’s!

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I was actually quite annoyed they killed off Chas.

But film is brilliant. 

Devil is a perfect casting.
  - I loved Tilda as well 
    - https://i.redd.it/j88olt4hphbh1.gif  
   
🔥🔥🔥
  - In the deleted scene Chas came back as his character in the comic is immortal hence why he is John's oldest friend, probably the only reason he is his oldest friend. That being said the movie didn't exactly stick close to the comic. I always say it was an awesome movie, but a bad adaptation.

  
Found the clip.

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JTrawhg-WGg](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JTrawhg-WGg)
2. I barely remember, but I don't think it was marketed in a way that prepared audiences for the ride we were about to be taken on.  Too much emphasis on effects, when the whole point of the movie was the mood.

Still a permanent fixture in my rotation though.
  - Yeah the marketing for this film was not great.

Everyone was thinking they were gonna get the Matrix but with demons.

Instead it’s a Neo noir slow burn about faith and redemption.
3. Peter Stormare pushes this movie from pretty damn good to awesome. Far and away my favorite rendition of Satan on screen.
  - https://preview.redd.it/pwfsll1pnhbh1.jpeg?width=1080&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=109da65ca8850745954c242232fdf6bf017884ac

THE GREATEST EVER 💯
4. I'm still hoping for a sequel 
  - https://www.imdb.com/news/ni65308157/

Supposedly Stormare is returning! Old article but worth looking into.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Now that its been a few years, how do you feel about Poor Things?

**Score:** 116
**Body:** Poor Things is a film that I've noticed IS VERY divisive among feminist circles. While some see it as a disturbing but powerful look at the way men mistreat and infantilize women. Others see it as blatant p\\\*do-bait disguising itself as high art.

I personally lean towards the former than the later. The film makes it so glaringly clear these men are gross and abusive that i think coming out of the film thinking its condoning their behavior has me scratching my head. It feels like a take that you have to strip away any and all nuance to arrive at.

I have seen some people leave the movie thinking its a film about sexual empowerment, which is definitely a take that misses the mark. It ignores the obvious issues of the ability to consent (shes obviously not of a "sound mind")

When Duncan tells Bella "you're starting to lose the sweet way you used to talk", I'd say that serves as the films mission statement. The more mature Bella gets the less desirable she becomes to the men around her. Yes its gross, yes its uncomfortable, yes it's disturbing, but thats the way the film wants you to feel. Its a complete condemnation of the way these men view women and treat Bella.

Now, im 100% willing to admit i could be blind to how the movie fails its messaging. But it's one of those cases where it feels like a lot of discourse strips nuance away and a more recent phenomenon in art discourse where people cant separate "a film making be uncomfortable" from a "film being bad"

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the biggest problem for all this controversy is that the movie only tells a part of the book that it's told by the men. the book, as I searched, end with a letter from Bella explaining that most of the story is romanticized/mysticized by the men who told it. maybe yorgos wanted this, but still
  - It also ends with the reveal that she wasn’t actually a baby implanted into an adult body. It was a hoax. And there was no sex. And it was a socialist allegory. Pretty much everything interesting in the book was stripped from the movie. 
    - The book takes the form of two documents, one written by McCandless and one by Bella. The latter completely refutes the former and I think the reader is meant to find Bella's letter more credible, but it is not necessarily conclusively resolved within the text. 

I'm not sure how it is a "socialist allegory." I think what the book and film share is the story of a woman who refuses to have her story told be others and refuses to conform to expectations of what she should be.
2. Im Scottish, the book is Scottish. I hate the middle class English bent the film takes. It was tone dead to change the location, and if he really wanted to keep the feminist tone of the book, he wouldn't have changed the ending. 
3. I wasn’t a fan how Bella seemed so moved by realizing that there were people living in poor conditions when she wandered off the cruise ship… But then her path afterward was to become a prostitute, and then, seemingly, living secluded as a happy rich woman.

I wish I could’ve seen more personal growth in her wanting to change the world in positive ways that didn’t involve her sexuality.
  - Yeah, I think the argument presented by OP (and most people writing about the film, tbf) is pretty reductive. I didn’t think that it glorified pedophilia - Bella is an adult woman with an adult body, and the men who tried to take advantage of her mental state were clearly meant to appear predatory, pathetic, or both. What really bothered me was the implication that sexual promiscuity is inherently empowering for women. It reduces her emancipation to sexual autonomy, while giving comparatively little attention to intellectual growth, meaningful relationships, creative achievement, political agency, or any of the other ways a person might find true freedom. The whole thing felt disappointingly third-wave feminist-y, especially coming from Lanthimos. He's usually so incisive and challenging that I expected something more complex than a fairly familiar liberal feminist message.
    - Completely agreed on everything. It felt very weird at the time of release to hear how feminist this film was and all I could think of was sure, maybe 50 years ago. 

Frankly, I find the whole thing rather insulting to women.
4. My original opinion holds; it's pretty, and so very shallow. It's not a film that explores ideas; it's a film that has characters mention ideas out loud and do nothing with that. It parades its own complacency under the guise of transgressiveness, but God forbid it might actually offend anyone.

And while I value the pictorial formalism it aims for, when the lens choice in a scene is more interesting than the scene itself, it becomes too precious.
5. I don’t think it’s “pedo bait” but I do think the sex scenes are incredibly repetitive and made the film a complete slog in the second half. Visually it’s great but Yorgos needed to get to the fucking point and have some actual shit happening. I also think the decision to change the ending of the book (which admittedly I haven’t read) is a questionable one. It’s a real mixed bag for me, I think it could have been my favorite Lanthimos work if it just felt less repetitive but as is, I think it’s one of his weaker efforts. 
6. I keep thinking about this one review of the movie I read somewhere on Reddit - why doesn’t Bella have any body hair? And I mean, sure, this can across as a facile critique but if the movie wasn’t meant to feed into the voyeuristic gaze of men while simultaneously attempting to make some sort of a reductive statement about women and their agency over their own bodies and sexuality, why indeed does Bella with hair that touches her toes, not have any body hair if not to appease the male gaze?

## 2. Why does Kurosawa feel so contemporary?

**Score:** 108
**Body:** I just watched Akira Kurosawa Stray Dog for the first time, and despite a few elements of the film, it feels really fresh and almost contemporary.

That’s something I’ve noticed with most of his films- they standout amongst other films from the 40s-60s as having some contemporary feeling quality about them. I don’t feel like I’m watching a black and white film from the 40s or 50s. 

I was blown away the first time I watched Seven Samurai, I had the sense that it could be made today and it wouldn’t feel out of place.

I’m curious what your guys’s thoughts are on this?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There’s a dynamism to Kurosawa’s movies that I think only really came to American cinema with the introduction of the Steadicam. You read stories of his crew frantically laying down and/or picking up dolly tracks, while his focus puller is heroically keeping the shot in focus through the whole camera move. IIRC, the shot of the dog with the hand in Yojimbo, while simple and straightforward in modern filmmaking, was super hard because the dog is coming towards the camera, but not steady and straight, so the camera has to track him laterally while staying in focus as he gets closer, which all had to be done manually. 

But even Kurosawa’s static shots are meticulously blocked to be interesting to the eye and brain. 

[Here’s](https://youtu.be/LC8KYm85zig?si=8h3eO2w_ouDWbrnO) a video about Kurosawa’s blocking in High and Low.

[Here’s](https://youtu.be/doaQC-S8de8?si=eUi4nCVTEMavNbKv) a well-known video about Kurosawa’s use of movement.
2. Maybe it's because a lot of his dialogue feels grounded and realistic. Even though High and Low has stylized blocking, the arguments and stressful moments feel real, like something you'd see if you were watching police officers in real life. 

Seven Samurai...it's just aged well. It has a lot of criticism of gender roles and misogyny and has well written female characters. 
  - Agree with this. The functional and realistic dialogue ages well. This is one of the criticisms I have with filmmakers like Nolan, whose dialogue and characters exist as exposition vehicles rather than people. His strength is more as a structural theorist than screenwriter or even director
3. Part of it too has to do with that Kurosawa was so influential on the New Hollywood guys where we get so much of the language of modern cinema from

Scorsese, Lucas, Spielberg, Coppola all talk about having seen Kurosawa and been blown away leading to them to borrowing both thematic ideas of his and adapting cinematographic techniques (if that’s a term)
4. It's hard to say what you're responding to. There's a certain wounded humanism floating just under the surface of Kurosawa's often bleak, cynical worldview - particularly one that regards institutions and systems with a great deal of skepticism, to say the least - that could well reflect contemporary attitudes. 

But that's hardly unique to K in mid-20th century filmmaking. I wonder, have you seen many other films from that period that aren't in a language you speak (assuming you're not a Japanese speaker)? Perhaps the advantage of his films is that they're not dated in the same way by outmoded language, customs, etc., the way films from your native language(s) might be.

All of which is to say, have you spent much time with the films of, e.g., Billy Wilder? He is \*immensely\* contemporary in his attitudes.
5. I guess that’s because he influenced cinema greatly and also inspired many directors. You watch newer movies which borrowed from him, then you watch Kurosawa and he seems modern, i.e. familiar. 
6. I recognise this feeling. When I started watching 'old' movies I was - very naive of me - surprised how these movies seemed to speak more directly, more knowingly to feelings I know than a lot of modern movies. This immediately gives these movies a feeling of still being 'of today'. I had the same feeling with seven samurai, but the feeling kinda got moved to the background by how the action did feel very dated, which was actually really fun!! 
7. Because he inspired a lot of people that came afterwards, his techniques were mass adopted and absorbed into the international filmmaking zeitgeist during the auteur period of the 70s
8. Like others have already said, he inspired many of great directors nowadays. Scorsese and Spielberg are probably the most notable one. Looking at Spielberg’s Disclosure day, there were a lot of Kurosawa’s influence in the way he directed. Though Scorsese may not be so obvious (because he doesn’t do oner to that extend) but you can clearly sees the inspiration from Kurosawa. Silence had many shots that reminding me of Kurosawa.

In short, Kurosawa inspired Spielberg & Scorsese who are inspiring many modern directors.
9. Because he elevated common relatable content.

He Adapted Shakespeare and Hammett multiple times.

Rashomon is a creative take on a love triangle.

Seven Samurai kind of gives Alamo vibes. Plus it is all about noble sacrifice.

## 3. Ikiru Proves You Are Still Living in 1952

**Score:** 19
**Body:** The film is about bureaucracy, an administrative system that organizes work within institutions such as governments and companies through a strict hierarchy, a precise division of responsibilities, and written rules. The system was originally created to prevent favoritism and ensure fairness and impartiality, but today it is more commonly associated with excessive complexity, endless procedures, and painfully slow processes.

The film summarizes this system brilliantly at the very beginning. A group of women arrives to file a complaint about sewage water leaking into their neighborhood. When they bring the complaint to the appropriate employee, he passes it to someone above him in the hierarchy. That person then instructs them to go to the engineering department because it's an engineering issue. The engineering department sends them to the health department because it's a sanitation issue. The health department sends them to the sewage department, then to the prevention department, then to the infectious diseases department, then to the roads department, and then to another department, and another, and another... until they finally reach the mayor, who sends them right back to the public affairs department, the very place where their complaint began.

The film also shines a light on the people who manage these bureaucratic procedures, portraying them as if they are trapped inside a giant machine that forces them to function like cogs within it. They have no life outside this enormous machine that keeps them moving. The narrator describes them as dead, always busy, yet accomplishing nothing of real value. One employee hopes another will fall so he can climb higher in the hierarchy, believing that promotion will somehow change his life. In reality, however, he is simply moving from one cog to another so that the machine can continue operating.

But what happens if you tell someone who has spent his entire life as a cog within that system that he has only six months left to live? What would he do then? When you realize that all the time you spent working, building relationships, getting married, and raising children was never truly for yourself, that none of it was what you genuinely wanted, and that you never found happiness through any of it, what do you do? What does happiness mean to you at that moment? And how can a single piece of news, delivered in a single minute, completely transform a person's life, their worldview, and everything they have ever done?

These ideas are presented masterfully through the performances, the cinematography, the dialogue, and the screenplay, all of which leave you constantly on the verge of tears. How can a film released in 1952 possess such emotional power? How did it manage to explain what bureaucracy can do to our lives, how it can manipulate them, and how a single illness can completely change our lives in just a matter of minutes?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think you could probably do an interesting analysis of Kurosawa's liberal sensibilities by comparing *Ikiru*, *High and Low,* and *The Bad Sleep Well*, since he has a curious disdain for public bureaucracy but is much more ambivalent about private corporations.

Nonetheless, I'm not so sure I agree with this part:

>When you realize that all the time you spent working, building relationships, getting married, and raising children was never truly for yourself, that none of it was what you genuinely wanted, and that you never found happiness through any of it, what do you do?

I'm not sure this is a good read on *Ikiru*. Watanabe at the beginning of the film isn't a diligent, self-sacrificing cog in a machine who has never thought for his own happiness, he's exactly the opposite - he's the epitome of the lazy bureaucrats the film criticizes, who only cares to sit in his office, collect his paycheck, and pretend to be busy. This is why his first two attempts to find happiness after his cancer diagnosis (hedonistic pleasure-seeking and then attempting to parasitically absorb the zest for life and vitality of his young friend by osmosis) fall flat, because they are just different manifestations of the same selfishness he's displayed his entire life. Watanabe ultimately finds fulfillment in finally doing his job and acting as a public servant, energetically becoming someone who acts for the happiness *of others* rather than merely acting to preserve himself. It's precisely through fulfilling his actual social function that he is theoretically supposed to live up to that he finds happiness.

Kurosawa's cynicism comes through in that his actions only really have an immediate effect and don't bring about any kind of broader change (the other public servants all proclaim that they are going to take after his example and then turn back into lazy bums the next day), but still, the positive outcome Watanabe achieves and his happiness before his death are only possible through proper compliance with the social role he occupies. It's not at all about acting true to oneself or for one's own immediate happiness.

You could compare and contrast *Seven Samurai* here (which is where Kurosawa starts to become suspicious of the ideas he endorsed in *Ikiru)* and his 60s films, where he totally reverses course and starts embracing the theme of individuals heroically fighting against their social role and expectation.
  - Beautifully written analysis. Thank you.
2. Bureaucracy helps protect integrity. When you have to document that everything was run through appropriate procedures, it's a lot harder to corrupt. A DOGE employee said he was surprised by [how little waste or fraud](https://www.npr.org/2025/06/02/nx-s1-5417994/former-doge-engineer-shares-his-experience-working-for-the-cost-cutting-unit) they found in the U.S. government. 

Jumping through bureaucratic hoops sucks. Living in a kleptocracy is a whole lot worse. 
  - My brother in law worked in reducing WFA, and he's a straight shooter. It doesn't surprise me in the least that he and others like him did their job, and did it well. It's that kind of government employee that we risk losing thanks to the current administration.
3. I don't see that Kurosawa's thoughts on bureaucracy are very central to this film. It's a film about mortality and personal values. It's called "To Live", not "To Push Papers Around". The important part is that Takashi Shimura's character finds no meaning or satisfaction in his work, until his revelation. There are all kinds of jobs that people do not find meaning in, apart from civil service. The story works equally well if the character worked at a boring job in the private sector.
  - I'm not sure I agree with this, since Kurosawa's films are generally very occupied with how people grapple with the social roles they find themselves in. Specifically with *Ikiru*, it's not that Watanabe does his job and finds it unfulfilling, it's that he doesn't do his job, and ultimately he finds happiness through actually accomplishing the purpose of his career (which is to serve the public good) for once in his life. If Watanabe was a private corpo it would be a very different film, because it would lack the context of the protagonist being someone with the responsibility to do good but who neglects that duty.
    - But Watanabe has his revelation because his "girlfriend" describes her satisfaction in working in a factory, producing toys that bring joy to children. Meaning and purpose and a sense that one's labor improves the world: these exist in all kinds of work.

## 4. Non-western cinema outside the EU/US/Cannes/Criterion circuit?

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Hi all, just watched Lav Diaz’s *Magellan*. It was slow and beautiful and the rustling leaves, lapping waves, tropical rains etc. had me leaving the cinema feeling peaceful and contemplative. But also weird, because why do I feel like this after seeing a movie about the horrors of colonialism? 

Which got me thinking- Lav Diaz as an example of how “slow cinema” is a genre with its own assumptions and common traits just like any other genre, but it seems to be seen as more “elevated” within the US/EU circuit (it’s slow, therefore difficult, therefore good) akin to eating your cinematic vegetables. Of course there are lots of non-western “slow” directors (Kiarostami, Weerasethakul, Tsai Ming-Liang, etc) but they always appeal to US/EU audiences (screen at Cannes, are on criterion, etc). As if these films were made always in response to the US/EU gaze, instead of completely separate from it. 

(This is why it was so jarring to me, coming out of Diaz’s film. He’s a Filipino director but it just seemed like this slow, European gaze was not the right tone for a movie about settler colonialism. Idk though, it is a bit more nuanced than that)

So my question is, what are examples of good cinema that falls outside of this US/EU circuit? For example, Bollywood, k-drama, Japanese pinku, etc are not really made with these audiences in mind, but their own. Any other good examples, films I should watch, things I should read?

a bit ironic since Diaz has discussed the issue of slowness with regard to Filipino pre-hispanic culture and temporality! anyway this seems off - the only plausible reading is that long takes and slow camera movements are in some sense essentially european, which is clearly absurd and a bit reductive towards non-european authors, who have developed their own language in relation to the same historical tradition than their western counterparts, so they shouldn't be expected to be "more removed" from it

 
  - They are not essentially European but they were developed in a European context, like the studio system was developed in the US. So I don't think it is wrong to say for eg that Lav Diaz is engaging w a form of photography which achieved prominence under some European filmmakers.

That being said, to reduce the films of Philippines or Thailand or India to their European content is to miss the forest for the trees. I think you are very right in saying that there are domestic historiographies which these filmmakers are engaging with.

## 5. I re-watched Comrades, Almost a Love Story (1996) dir. Peter Chan recently, and was wondering about the cultural context of the settings

**Score:** 8
**Body:** I really adore this movie, and I'm hoping to understand more about it, beyond just seeing these charming characters find their way to being with each other. Maggie Cheung and Leon Lai are really wonderful with the way they express themselves, but what really fascinated me were how their characters - Li Qiao, and Li Xiao-Jun, came from specific parts of mainland China. Li Qiao came from Guangzhou, which she says has similarities to Hong Kong. Whereas Li Xiao-Jun was a northerner, who seems simpler in nature, contrasting Li Qiao's (and perhaps Hong Kong's?) more financially-centered goals to build wealth.

They both seem to find a deep connection within Teresa Teng's music, and Li Qiao, and other immigrants from the mainland hide their ties to avoid being othered. I could tell that within this more capitalist driven environment, Teresa Teng's songs really stood out as sincere, and sweet. There are some gaps in my understanding, since I am not from Hong Kong or China, and I'd really like to understand the deeper aspects of this story. Was there meant to be a parallel between the complicated turns in their romance, and Hong Kong & mainland China? I know certain directors like Wong Kar-Wai, and Hou Hsiao-hsien have done this in their movies and their countries, which is why I noticed it.

I'm also wondering if there was any significance in the US being the setting of the last act, given it might be more capitalist in nature compared to so many other parts of the world. It's interesting how something simple yet good like Teresa Teng's music playing on the news, is what ended up uniting them, even though the chances in such an environment are low. Really hoping to hear more of everyone's thoughts about this! Thanks in advance :)

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I am not Chinese but the person I watched it was is Taiwanese so we talked a bit about this. But forgive me if I am missing details. In 1949 China had its own revolution, where they expelled all the capitalists, aiming for a communist country. Because of this, a lot of (now former) capitalist citizens went to Hong Kong and Taiwan. This is also what Farewell My Concubine and A Brighter Summer Day are largely about. So this is the premise of the film: China is communist and HK is capitalist. You to go to HK to “make it big,” something much akin to the American Dream. 

I actually think the film is critiquing capitalism, as Maggie Cheung isn’t able to just enjoy life and fall in love, since she is too busy on the capitalist grind. So this is why I agree with you that the movie decided to end in NY because it is (at least colloquially considered) more capitalist. 
  - Wow, I see. Yeah it was evident that Maggie Cheung's character internally struggled with her feelings for Xiao-Jun, from her focus on the grind. I love the way it critiqued capitalism, it's more relevant than ever. Thank you for mentioning the bit about the revolution in 1949, that is fascinating!
    - No worries. This is why the flag of China pre-revolution is the same flag that Taiwan currently uses. Funnily, Teresa Tang is actually from Taiwan. I believe her career really exploded in China, so when they are saying in the movie that it’s primarily mainlanders that like her, I think it’s accurate. 
      - Wow she's actually from Taiwan! Appreciate that, there's really a lot to learn about the culture of these eras
        - Indeed!
2. Teresa Teng was loved in all the places Chinese people were. She was popular in Malaysia, Indonesia, Singapore, Taiwan, Hong Kong and in the USA (where she lived for a couple of years in between 1979 and 1982). As she spoke and sang in several languages, she was also popular in Japan where from 1974-1991, she released several Japanese language albums that were big hits. 

She was born in Taiwan in 1953 but considered herself both Taiwanese and Chinese. She was a first generation Taiwanese person as her folks were mainlanders that fled China in 1949 and like her family, considered Taiwan to be part of China. This is why a lot of younger pro-independence Taiwanese don't like her because of that stance. But her belief that Taiwan and China are one stems from being born in that era. Taiwan was ruled by the KMT until the 1990's and they not only believed Taiwan was a part of China, they also expected the communist government in China to collapse and the KMT would once again rule China and Taiwan under the name Republic Of China (ROC). This is why Taiwan's formal name is Republic of China. Taiwan's rulers and people who were mainlanders thought the stay in Taiwan would be short.

I am a big fan of Teresa Teng and while I like her later stuff, I actually like her early stuff from 1967-1974 a lot better. Most folks know her from ballads like The Moon Represents My Heart but a lot of folks don't remember her early stuff which was more diverse and seemed to explore many genres and seemed to favor more bass and organ work in the songs.
